title: practical english usage - Part 369

For similar structures with if, In informal speech, sentences like I wish you'd have seen it sometimes occur.

Now she wishes she had studied harder.

I wish you hadn't said that. (= It would be nice if you hadn’ t said that.) Past perfect tenses are used for wishes about the past.

I wish that I were better looking.

Were can be used instead of was in this structure, especially in a formal style.

Do you ever wish you lived somewhere else?

All the staff wish you weren't leaving so soon.

I wish I had a yacht. I wish tomorrow was Sunday.

I wish I spoke Japanese. (= It would be nice if I spoke Japanese.) present or future meaning.

for instance, after 'It would be nice if...’  (►  239). Past tenses are used with a In a Zhflt-clause after wish, we  generally use the same tenses as we  would use, tenses 4  wish     + that-clause: not I hope you feel better tomorrow, (     I wish you felt better tomorrow?) not I hope you pass your exams, (     I wish you would pass your exams.) possible in the future. We   often use hope in this sense (►  490).

Wish + that-clause is not generally used for wishes about things that seem We all wish (that) the snow would stay forever.

Don't you wish (that) you could fly?

I wish (that) I was better looking.

Tenses are similar to those used with if (see below).

are not different, and refers to situations that are unreal, impossible or unlikely.

style). In this case, wish does not mean 'want' - it expresses regret that things We   can also use wish with a thɑt-dause (that can be dropped in an informal meaning 3  wish     + that-clause: Word  Problems from A  to Z     Section     31 vocabulary •  633    with For the difference between by and with, ►  i 1 6.

Note also: by car/train, etc (     wifh-the-car, etc), and write in pencil!ink.

Why are you talking in such a loud voice?

We   say in a ... voice,   with-a -.. . voice.

Could you go and give this paper to the woman in glasses?

Who's the man in the funny hat?

Note that in is often used instead of with to refer to articles of clothing.

6 clothing,      voices,     transport,       etc They've bought a house with a big garden.

There are so many people around with no homes. (= ... who  have no homes.) With is very often used, like have, to indicate possession and similar ideas.

Without Emma  and Jake, we're going to have trouble finishing the repairs.

Without can be used in similar ways.

With friends like you, who needs enemies?

With all this work to do, I won't have time to go out.

The runners started the race with a light following wind.

and there is/was or because there is/was).

With can introduce accompanying circumstances or reasons (rather like 4 accompanying          circumstances          and   reasons Will you play chess with me?

Don't fight with him  - he's bigger than you are.

with can be used with the same meaning as against.

After fight, struggle, quarrel, argue, play and words with similar meanings, 3  with     meaning       'against' not She was very nice to me. (     .. ,-niee-with-me:) rude, good), we  generally use to, not with.

After words which  say how  people act towards others (like kind, nice, polite, angry with      pleased with I'm cross with you. furious with upset with towards others.

With is also used after a number of adjectives which  say how  people are feeling 2  angry       with,     etc red with anger/embarrassment  shivering with cold white with fear/rage       green with envy When I found her she was blue with cold, Annie was jumping up and down with excitement.

My father was trembling with rage.

their emotions and sensations.

With is used in a number of expressions which  say how  people are showing 1  trembling           with     rage,     blue     with     cold,     etc 633 with vocahulaiy •  634   worth (= . .. I’ ll pay you enough.)                                                                             -> Would you like to do some gardening for me? I'll make it worth your while.
